What does DRS stand for?
Drag Reduction System
What does ERS stand for?
Energy Recovery System
How many points does a race win score?
How many points for the fastest lap?
1 — but only if you finish in the top 10
How many drivers score points in a Grand Prix?
The top 10
What colour flag signals the end of a session?
Chequered
What does a blue flag tell a driver?
Let a faster, lapping car through
What does a black-and-orange flag mean?
Mechanical problem — return to the pits
What does a yellow flag mean?
Danger ahead — slow down, no overtaking
What does a red flag mean?
Session stopped
What's the minimum age for an F1 super licence?
How many super licence points are needed for an F1 seat?
40, earned over three seasons
What tyre colour marks the softest dry compound?
Red
What tyre colour marks the hardest dry compound?
White
What colour is the intermediate wet tyre?
Green
What colour is the full wet tyre?
Blue
How many dry tyre compounds does Pirelli bring to a race weekend?
Three — hard, medium and soft
Must a driver use two different tyre compounds in a dry race?
Yes
What's the maximum number of cars on an F1 grid?
26, though grids are smaller today
How many teams currently race in F1?
How many cars does each team field?
Two
What engine formula does F1 use in 2025?
1.6-litre V6 turbo hybrid
Roughly how many horsepower does a modern F1 power unit produce?
Around 1000 hp
What's the minimum weight of an F1 car (with driver)?
798 kg for 2025
What's KERS, from the early hybrid era?
Kinetic Energy Recovery System
What does 'pole position' mean?
First place on the starting grid
What sets the grid order?
Qualifying
How many parts does qualifying have?
Three — Q1, Q2 and Q3
How many cars are knocked out after Q1?
The slowest five
What is a 'formation lap'?
The lap to the grid before the race start
What is 'parc fermé'?
Restricted conditions where car setup is locked
What's an 'undercut' in strategy?
Pitting earlier than a rival to jump them on fresh tyres
What's an 'overcut'?
Staying out longer than a rival to jump them
What's a 'lock-up'?
Braking hard enough to stop a wheel and flat-spot the tyre
What's 'graining' on a tyre?
Surface rubber tearing and rolling, costing grip
What's the 'delta' a driver follows under a Safety Car?
A minimum lap time they must not beat
What is a 'Virtual Safety Car'?
A neutralisation where drivers hold a set delta, no physical car deployed
What's 'dirty air'?
Turbulent air behind a car that cuts the following car's downforce
What's 'porpoising'?
Aerodynamic bouncing of the car at speed
What's the 'racing line'?
The fastest path through a corner
What's an 'apex'?
The inner point of a corner the car aims for
Who has won the most F1 World Championships?
Hamilton and Schumacher — 7 each
Who won the very first F1 World Championship, in 1950?
Giuseppe Farina
Which team has won the most Constructors' Championships?
Ferrari
Which is the only team to have contested every F1 season?
Ferrari
Who is the youngest driver ever to win a Grand Prix?
Max Verstappen — 18 years, 228 days (2016 Spain)
Who is the youngest World Champion?
Sebastian Vettel — 23 (2010)
Who has taken the most career pole positions?
Lewis Hamilton — over 100
Who scored F1's first Constructors' title, in 1958?
Vanwall
Which British team is based at a former RAF airfield?
Several — the sport's heart is 'Motorsport Valley' in England
Which driver is nicknamed 'The Professor'?
Alain Prost
Which driver won four straight titles from 2010 to 2013?
Sebastian Vettel
Which driver won five straight titles from 2000 to 2004?
Michael Schumacher
Who was the first driver to win a title with a hybrid-era car?
Lewis Hamilton (2014)
Which country has hosted the most Grands Prix?
Italy
What's the oldest event on the F1 calendar?
The British Grand Prix — part of the first 1950 season
Which Grand Prix is famous for its harbour and street circuit?
Monaco
Which circuit features the Eau Rouge–Raidillon complex?
Spa-Francorchamps
Which circuit has the corner called '130R'?
Suzuka
Which circuit ends with the long 'Parabolica'?
Monza
Which circuit is nicknamed 'The Temple of Speed'?
Monza
Which circuit has the Maggotts–Becketts–Chapel sequence?
Silverstone
What's unusual about Suzuka's layout?
It's a figure-of-eight — the track crosses over itself
Which circuit has the banked final corner at Zandvoort?
The Arie Luyendyk bend
Which street circuit is the slowest on the calendar?
Monaco
Which circuit hosts the fastest average lap speeds?
Monza
Which corner at Monaco is the tightest hairpin in F1?
The Fairmont (Loews) Hairpin
Which circuit's first corner is called 'Senna S'?
Interlagos
Which new circuit joins the calendar for 2026?
Madrid
Which circuit runs anticlockwise and tests drivers' necks?
Interlagos (and a few others)
Roughly how long is a standard F1 race?
About 305 km (Monaco is shorter)
What's the maximum race time before it's stopped?
Three hours
How many laps is the Monaco Grand Prix?
What's the fastest recorded speed in an F1 race, roughly?
Over 370 km/h
How quickly can a top F1 pit crew change four tyres?
Under two seconds
How many gears does a modern F1 gearbox have?
Eight forward
How do F1 drivers change gear?
Paddles behind the steering wheel
About how many G can a driver pull in heavy braking?
Around 5G
How much can a driver's weight seem to increase under 5G?
Five times — a big load on the neck
What's the fuel-flow limit designed to cap?
Engine power, by restricting fuel per hour
Can F1 cars refuel during a race?
No — refuelling has been banned since 2010
What material are F1 chassis mostly made from?
Carbon fibre
What's the survival cell around the driver called?
The monocoque
What's the 'halo'?
The titanium cockpit protection device above the driver
When was the halo introduced?
What's a 'sausage kerb'?
A raised kerb designed to deter cutting corners
What's the DRS 'zone'?
A track section where the rear wing can be opened to overtake
What condition lets a driver use DRS in the race?
Being within one second of the car ahead at the detection point
What does the steering-wheel display mostly show a driver?
Live data — delta, gears, energy, tyre and brake info
What's a 'brake bias' adjustment?
Shifting braking effort between the front and rear axles
What's 'engine braking'?
Using the engine's resistance to slow the car
What are 'marbles' off the racing line?
Bits of worn tyre rubber that offer little grip
What's a 'box' call on the radio?
An instruction to pit
Where does the term 'box' come from?
The German 'Boxenstopp' — pit stop
What's a 'stop-and-go' penalty?
Pit, stop for a set time, no work done, then rejoin
What's a 'drive-through' penalty?
Drive through the pit lane at the limit without stopping
What's the usual pit-lane speed limit?
60 or 80 km/h depending on the circuit
What's a 'grand slam' in F1?
Pole, win, fastest lap and leading every lap in one race
What's a 'lights-out' start?
Race begins the instant the five red lights go out
How long can the start lights hold before going out?
Between one and a few seconds — deliberately unpredictable
What is the 'sprint' format?
A short Saturday race that also sets part of the weekend
Which flag warns of a slippery track?
Yellow-and-red striped
What does a white flag on track indicate?
A slow vehicle ahead
Who leads the race on the timing screens as 'P1'?
The car currently in first place
What's an 'installation lap'?
A slow lap to check systems before running properly
What's 'tyre degradation'?
The gradual loss of grip and pace as a tyre wears
What's the 'cost cap'?
A limit on how much a team can spend per season
What are 'tokens' in engine development?
A now-retired system limiting in-season engine changes
What does 'DNF' mean in results?
Did Not Finish
What does 'DNS' mean?
Did Not Start
What does 'DSQ' mean?
Disqualified
What's a 'lapped' car?
One that the leaders are a full lap ahead of
What's a 'blue-flag' offence?
Ignoring the flag and blocking a lapping car
What's the constructors' prize based on?
Combined points of a team's two cars across the season
